seeds.prompt: A circular playground has a radius of 14 m. A concrete path of uniform width 3.5 m is constructed around the outside of the playground. (i) Find the area of the concrete path. (ii) If the concrete costs ₹80 per square metre, find the total cost of laying the path. Use π = 22/7. Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Outer radius = 14 + 3.5 = 17.5 m. Area of outer circle = (22/7) × 17.5² = (22/7) × 306.25 = 962.5 m². Area of inner circle (playground) = (22/7) × 14² = (22/7) × 196 = 616 m². Area of path = 962.5 − 616 = 346.5 m². Total cost = 346.5 × 80 = ₹27,720.

metadata.angle: geometry and mensuration in real scenarios (fields, tiles, tanks — areas, perimeters, angles)

seeds.prompt: A farmer owns an L-shaped plot of land. The plot fits inside a bounding rectangle of 80 m × 60 m, but a rectangular section of 30 m × 20 m has been cut away from one corner. (i) Find the area of the L-shaped plot. (ii) Find the total length of fencing needed to enclose the entire plot. Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Area of bounding rectangle = 80 × 60 = 4800 m². Area of cut-away corner = 30 × 20 = 600 m². Area of L-shaped plot = 4800 − 600 = 4200 m². The L-shaped boundary has six sides. Cutting the corner reduces one pair of sides: the 60 m side becomes 40 m and the 80 m side becomes 50 m, while the cutaway introduces two new interior edges of 20 m and 30 m. Perimeter = 80 + 40 + 30 + 20 + 50 + 60 = 280 m.

metadata.angle: geometry and mensuration in real scenarios (fields, tiles, tanks — areas, perimeters, angles)

seeds.prompt: A closed rectangular water storage tank is 5 m long, 3 m wide, and 2 m tall. The entire outer surface of the tank — all six faces including the base and lid — is to be painted. The painter charges ₹35 per square metre. What is the total cost of painting? Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Total surface area of a closed rectangular box = 2(lb + bh + lh), where l = 5 m, b = 3 m, h = 2 m. Surface area = 2(5×3 + 3×2 + 5×2) = 2(15 + 6 + 10) = 2 × 31 = 62 m². Total cost = 62 × 35 = ₹2,170.

metadata.angle: geometry and mensuration in real scenarios (fields, tiles, tanks — areas, perimeters, angles)

seeds.prompt: A plot of land is shaped as a quadrilateral PQRS. A surveyor draws the diagonal PR and measures it to be 60 m. The perpendicular distance from vertex Q to line PR is 25 m, and the perpendicular distance from vertex S to line PR is 15 m. Vertices Q and S lie on opposite sides of PR. Find the total area of plot PQRS. Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Diagonal PR divides quadrilateral PQRS into two triangles: △PQR and △PSR. Area of △PQR = ½ × base × height = ½ × 60 × 25 = 750 m². Area of △PSR = ½ × 60 × 15 = 450 m². Total area of PQRS = 750 + 450 = 1200 m².

metadata.angle: geometry and mensuration in real scenarios (fields, tiles, tanks — areas, perimeters, angles)

seeds.prompt: A rectangular courtyard is 10 m long and 8 m wide. It is to be tiled completely with square tiles each of side 0.5 m. The outermost ring of tiles (the border) will be red, and all remaining interior tiles will be white. (i) Find the total number of tiles needed. (ii) How many tiles will be red? (iii) How many tiles will be white? Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Tiles along length = 10 ÷ 0.5 = 20. Tiles along width = 8 ÷ 0.5 = 16. (i) Total tiles = 20 × 16 = 320. (ii) Removing the border on all four sides leaves an interior grid of (20 − 2) × (16 − 2) = 18 × 14 = 252 white tiles. (iii) Red border tiles = 320 − 252 = 68. Verification: top row = 20, bottom row = 20, left column excluding corners = 14, right column excluding corners = 14; border total = 20 + 20 + 14 + 14 = 68. ✓

metadata.angle: geometry and mensuration in real scenarios (fields, tiles, tanks — areas, perimeters, angles)

Without listing all individual divisors of 496, determine the sum of ALL its divisors including 496 itself. Show your reasoning step by step.

seeds.prompt: January 1, 2024 is a Monday, and 2024 is a leap year. What day of the week is March 15, 2024? Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: March 15, 2024 is a Friday. From January 1 to March 15 is 30 (remaining Jan days) + 29 (Feb, leap year) + 15 (Mar 1–15) = 74 days after January 1. 74 ÷ 7 = 10 weeks remainder 4. Monday + 4 days = Friday.

metadata.angle: calendar, clock, and time-zone reasoning

seeds.prompt: A team meeting is set for 9:00 PM IST (India Standard Time, UTC+5:30) on a Tuesday. One colleague is in Tokyo (JST, UTC+9) and another is in San Francisco (PDT, UTC−7). For each colleague, what is their local time and local calendar day when the meeting starts? Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Convert 9:00 PM IST to UTC: 21:00 − 5:30 = 15:30 UTC (Tuesday). Tokyo (UTC+9): 15:30 + 9:00 = 24:30 → 12:30 AM JST on Wednesday (the next calendar day). San Francisco (UTC−7): 15:30 − 7:00 = 8:30 AM PDT on Tuesday (the same calendar day). The Tokyo colleague joins on Wednesday; the San Francisco colleague joins on Tuesday.

metadata.angle: calendar, clock, and time-zone reasoning

seeds.prompt: A child is born on February 29, 2000. By December 31, 2024, how many times has February 29 appeared on a calendar after the child's birth year — that is, how many leap-day anniversaries has the child had? List the years. Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: A year has February 29 only if it is a leap year (divisible by 4, with century-year exceptions). The leap years after 2000 up to and including 2024 are: 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020, and 2024. That is 6 leap-day anniversaries.

metadata.angle: calendar, clock, and time-zone reasoning

seeds.prompt: January 1, 2025 is a Wednesday. 2025 is not a leap year, so February 2025 has 28 days. How many Fridays are there in total across January and February 2025 combined? List the dates. Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: January 1 is Wednesday, so the first Friday is January 3. Fridays in January: 3, 10, 17, 24, 31 — that is 5 Fridays. January 31 is Friday, so February 1 is Saturday. The first Friday in February is February 7. Fridays in February: 7, 14, 21, 28 — that is 4 Fridays. Total: 5 + 4 = 9 Fridays across the two months.

metadata.angle: calendar, clock, and time-zone reasoning

seeds.prompt: A flight departs Mumbai (IST, UTC+5:30) at 11:45 PM on March 14. The flight duration is 3 hours and 20 minutes. The destination is Dubai, which observes Gulf Standard Time (GST, UTC+4). What is the local arrival time and date in Dubai? Show your reasoning step by step.
seeds.expected: Departure in UTC: 23:45 IST − 5h 30m = 18:15 UTC on March 14. Add flight duration: 18:15 + 3h 20m = 21:35 UTC on March 14. Convert to GST (UTC+4): 21:35 + 4h 00m = 25:35, which is 01:35 AM on March 15. The flight arrives at 1:35 AM GST on March 15.

metadata.angle: calendar, clock, and time-zone reasoning

seeds.prompt: Many migratory birds, such as the European robin, navigate thousands of kilometers each year with remarkable precision. Scientists have found that these birds possess specialized proteins called cryptochromes located in their eyes, which are sensitive to the Earth's magnetic field. When light strikes these proteins, a chemical reaction produces pairs of electrons whose spin states are influenced by geomagnetic field lines, allowing the bird to perceive magnetic north as a kind of visual signal. This biological compass remains functional even on overcast nights when stars are not visible. Laboratory experiments that block this magnetic sense have confirmed that affected birds lose their directional accuracy.
seeds.expected: Migratory birds navigate using light-sensitive proteins in their eyes that detect the Earth's magnetic field and function as an internal biological compass.

metadata.angle: a science discovery or natural phenomenon

seeds.prompt: In 1977, oceanographers using a deep-sea submersible discovered thriving communities of organisms clustered around hydrothermal vents on the floor of the Pacific Ocean, nearly 2,500 meters below the surface. Unlike almost all other known ecosystems, these communities do not depend on sunlight for energy. Instead, chemosynthetic bacteria convert hydrogen sulfide released by the vents into organic matter, forming the base of a food web that supports tube worms, clams, and shrimp. The discovery expanded scientific understanding of where life can exist and has direct implications for the search for life on other planets with subsurface oceans. These vent communities have remained largely stable for decades despite the extreme heat and pressure of their surroundings.
seeds.expected: Hydrothermal vent ecosystems, discovered in 1977, are powered by chemosynthesis rather than sunlight, demonstrating that life can sustain itself in extreme, lightless environments.

metadata.angle: a science discovery or natural phenomenon

seeds.prompt: All elements heavier than hydrogen and helium — including carbon, oxygen, iron, and gold — were produced inside stars through a process called stellar nucleosynthesis, in which nuclear fusion reactions combine lighter atomic nuclei into heavier ones. When massive stars exhaust their fuel, they explode as supernovae, ejecting these newly formed elements into surrounding space. Over billions of years, this material is incorporated into gas clouds that condense into new stars and planetary systems. The calcium in bones and the iron in blood were created inside stars that exploded long before the solar system formed roughly 4.6 billion years ago. This process is now a foundational result of nuclear astrophysics, supported by both theoretical models and the observed composition of stars across the universe.
seeds.expected: All elements heavier than hydrogen and helium, including those essential to living organisms, were forged inside stars through nuclear fusion and dispersed into space when those stars exploded as supernovae.

metadata.angle: a science discovery or natural phenomenon

seeds.prompt: Beneath the soil of most forests lies a vast network of fungal threads called mycorrhizae, which form associations with the roots of roughly 90 percent of land plant species. These fungi extend the effective root surface area of host plants enormously, helping trees absorb water and nutrients such as phosphorus that roots alone cannot easily reach. In return, the plants supply the fungi with sugars produced through photosynthesis. Research has shown that mature trees can transfer carbon and nitrogen to younger seedlings through this shared network, supporting forest regeneration after disturbance. The system connects individual trees across large areas and is thought to play a role in the long-term resilience of forest ecosystems.
seeds.expected: Mycorrhizal fungal networks connect the roots of most land plants, enabling the exchange of nutrients and carbon between trees and contributing to forest regeneration and resilience.

metadata.angle: a science discovery or natural phenomenon

seeds.prompt: Bioluminescence — the production of visible light by living organisms — occurs across a wide range of species, including certain bacteria, fungi, jellyfish, and deep-sea fish. The light is generated through a chemical reaction in which a molecule called luciferin is oxidized in the presence of an enzyme called luciferase, releasing energy as light with very little accompanying heat. In the deep ocean, where sunlight does not penetrate, an estimated three-quarters of animal species are bioluminescent, using light to attract prey, locate mates, or deter predators. Bioluminescence is estimated to have evolved independently at least 94 separate times across different branches of the tree of life, making it one of the most widespread examples of convergent evolution. Researchers have also adapted the luciferase gene as a tool in biomedical research to track cellular processes.
seeds.expected: Bioluminescence, produced by a chemical reaction between luciferin and luciferase, has evolved independently dozens of times and is especially prevalent in the deep ocean, where it serves functions such as predation, communication, and defense.

metadata.angle: a science discovery or natural phenomenon

seeds.prompt: Krishnadevaraya ruled the Vijayanagara Empire in southern India from 1509 to 1529 and is considered one of the greatest monarchs of the medieval Deccan. Under his reign, the empire reached its greatest territorial extent, controlling large parts of present-day Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu. He was also a celebrated poet in the Telugu language, composing the literary work Amuktamalyada. His court attracted scholars, poets, and artists from across the subcontinent, making the capital Hampi a center of learning and architecture. He also maintained diplomatic and trade relations with Portuguese merchants who had recently arrived on the western coast of India.
seeds.expected: Krishnadevaraya, who ruled the Vijayanagara Empire from 1509 to 1529, was both a powerful military king who brought the empire to its greatest extent and a celebrated Telugu poet whose court became a major center of art and scholarship.

metadata.angle: a historical event or figure (Indian or world)

seeds.prompt: The Great Fire of London began in September 1666 in a bakery on Pudding Lane and spread rapidly through the city's closely packed wooden buildings over four days. It destroyed approximately 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and many civic buildings across an area of about 1.6 square kilometers. Despite the enormous scale of destruction, the recorded death toll was surprisingly low, with only a small number of confirmed fatalities. The disaster prompted a large rebuilding effort, and the architect Christopher Wren designed the new St. Paul's Cathedral along with dozens of other churches to replace those lost. The fire also ended the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in the city.
seeds.expected: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days, destroying thousands of houses and dozens of churches, and led to a major rebuilding of the city including Christopher Wren's new St. Paul's Cathedral.

metadata.angle: a historical event or figure (Indian or world)

seeds.prompt: In 1905, the British colonial administration under Viceroy Lord Curzon divided the Bengal Presidency into two separate provinces — East Bengal with Assam, and West Bengal. The stated reason was administrative convenience, as Bengal was the most populous province in British India, but many Indians believed the partition was designed to weaken Bengali nationalism by splitting the region along religious lines, with Muslims in the majority in the east and Hindus in the west. The division triggered the Swadeshi movement, in which Indians boycotted British manufactured goods and promoted Indian-produced alternatives. Public meetings, street protests, and the burning of foreign cloth became widespread across Bengal. The partition was reversed in 1911 under pressure from the nationalist movement.
seeds.expected: The 1905 partition of Bengal by the British administration under Lord Curzon, widely seen as an attempt to divide nationalist sentiment along religious lines, provoked the Swadeshi movement — a mass boycott of British goods that became a landmark episode in India's independence struggle.

metadata.angle: a historical event or figure (Indian or world)

seeds.prompt: Hypatia was a philosopher and mathematician who lived in Alexandria in the late fourth and early fifth centuries CE. She was a prominent teacher of Neoplatonic philosophy and made contributions to mathematics and astronomy by writing commentaries on works by Ptolemy and Diophantus. As a woman who held a leading public intellectual position in an era dominated by men, she attracted both admiration and hostility. In 415 CE she was killed by a mob in Alexandria during a period of violent conflict between civic and religious factions in the city. Her death is frequently cited by historians as a marker of the decline of classical learning in the ancient Mediterranean world.
seeds.expected: Hypatia, a fifth-century Alexandrian philosopher and mathematician and one of the most prominent female scholars of antiquity, was killed by a mob in 415 CE during a period of civic unrest, an event historians often associate with the broader decline of classical learning.

metadata.angle: a historical event or figure (Indian or world)

seeds.prompt: The Panama Canal, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through the Isthmus of Panama, was built by the United States and opened to shipping in August 1914 after roughly ten years of construction. The project required removing approximately 170 million cubic meters of earth and presented enormous engineering challenges. An earlier French attempt in the 1880s had failed in part because of severe outbreaks of malaria and yellow fever that killed tens of thousands of workers. American physician William Gorgas led the sanitation campaign that brought these diseases under control and made the American effort viable. The canal shortened the sea voyage between the two oceans by approximately 12,800 kilometers compared with sailing around the southern tip of South America.
seeds.expected: The Panama Canal, opened in 1914 after about ten years of construction led by the United States, connected the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans and succeeded where an earlier French attempt had failed, largely because of an effective public health campaign against malaria and yellow fever.

metadata.angle: a historical event or figure (Indian or world)

seeds.prompt: The Indian monsoon is driven by the temperature difference between the land and the surrounding ocean. During summer, the Indian subcontinent heats faster than the Indian Ocean, causing air over land to rise and forming a low-pressure zone. Moisture-carrying winds from the ocean move inland to replace that rising air. As these winds climb over the Western Ghats and Himalayan foothills, they cool rapidly and the moisture condenses into rain. This seasonal wind reversal delivers roughly 80 percent of India's annual rainfall between June and September.
seeds.expected: The Indian monsoon forms when the summer land-sea temperature difference draws moist ocean winds inland, where they rise, cool, and release heavy seasonal rainfall.

metadata.angle: a how-something-works / process explainer

seeds.prompt: A vaccine works by presenting the immune system with a harmless version of a pathogen, such as an inactivated form, a protein fragment, or a genetic blueprint for producing one. The immune system responds by generating antibodies and, critically, storing memory cells that recognize that pathogen. If the vaccinated person later encounters the actual pathogen, these memory cells enable a rapid, targeted immune response before serious illness can develop. Some vaccines require multiple doses spaced weeks apart to build durable immunity. This mechanism has allowed diseases such as smallpox and polio to be greatly reduced or eliminated.
seeds.expected: Vaccines protect against disease by training the immune system to recognize a pathogen through memory cells and antibodies, enabling a fast response if the real pathogen is encountered later.

metadata.angle: a how-something-works / process explainer

seeds.prompt: Ordinary glass is made by heating a mixture of silica sand, soda ash, and limestone to temperatures above 1,500 degrees Celsius until the materials fuse into a molten liquid. The molten glass can then be shaped by blowing, pressing, or pouring it into molds depending on the product being made. As it cools, the molecules do not arrange into a regular crystal lattice but solidify into a disordered, amorphous structure. Controlled slow cooling, a step called annealing, removes internal stresses that would otherwise cause the glass to crack. The result is a transparent, hard solid that can be formed into sheets, containers, or lenses.
seeds.expected: Glass is produced by melting silica sand with soda ash and limestone, shaping the molten material, and slowly cooling it through annealing to form a strong, stress-free amorphous solid.

metadata.angle: a how-something-works / process explainer

seeds.prompt: When a customer initiates a payment through a mobile application such as UPI, the app generates an encrypted request containing the payer's account details and the transaction amount. This request travels to a payment network, which routes it to the payer's bank for authentication and authorization. The bank verifies the account credentials and confirms that sufficient funds are available, then sends an approval signal back through the network. The payee's bank receives this confirmation and credits the agreed amount to the merchant's account. The full sequence typically completes within a few seconds.
seeds.expected: A mobile payment works by routing an encrypted authorization request from the payer's bank through a payment network to the payee's bank, which credits the merchant after the payer's credentials and balance are verified.

metadata.angle: a how-something-works / process explainer

seeds.prompt: A municipal water treatment plant purifies raw water from rivers or reservoirs through a series of stages before it enters household pipes. First, chemicals such as alum are added to make fine suspended particles bind together into heavier clumps called flocs, a process called coagulation. The water then moves into settling tanks where the flocs sink to the bottom through sedimentation. The clarified water passes through layers of sand and gravel that trap any remaining fine particles and microorganisms. Finally, a controlled dose of chlorine or another disinfectant is added to kill pathogens before the treated water enters the distribution network.
seeds.expected: Municipal water treatment purifies raw water by applying coagulation, sedimentation, filtration, and disinfection in sequence to remove particles and kill pathogens before the water reaches homes.

metadata.angle: a how-something-works / process explainer

seeds.prompt: Microfinance institutions in South Asia extend small loans, typically between five hundred and five thousand dollars, to low-income borrowers who lack access to formal banking. In India, self-help groups channel these loans primarily to rural women, who use the capital to start or expand small businesses such as tailoring, livestock rearing, and food processing. Studies tracking borrowers over five years show that households with microfinance access report higher income and lower dependence on informal moneylenders charging interest rates above 50 percent annually. Critics argue, however, that some microfinance programs have led borrowers into debt cycles when repayment schedules do not align with seasonal income patterns common in agriculture.
seeds.expected: Microfinance institutions in South Asia provide small loans to low-income borrowers, especially rural women, with documented income benefits but also risks of debt cycles from misaligned repayment schedules.

metadata.angle: an economic, social, or public-policy phenomenon

seeds.prompt: India's Goods and Services Tax, introduced in July 2017, replaced more than a dozen central and state indirect taxes with a single tiered structure. The reform aimed to eliminate the cascading effect of taxes levied at multiple production stages, which had added an estimated two to four percent to the cost of manufactured goods. In the first two years after implementation, tax registrations among small businesses increased by roughly fifty percent as previously informal traders entered the formal economy to access input-tax credits. However, compliance costs rose sharply for small and medium enterprises that lacked the accounting systems needed to file monthly returns under the new digital portal.
seeds.expected: India's 2017 GST reform unified indirect taxes and expanded formal-sector registration, but also raised compliance costs for small businesses without adequate accounting infrastructure.

metadata.angle: an economic, social, or public-policy phenomenon

seeds.prompt: Labor remittances — money sent home by workers employed abroad — represent the largest source of external financial inflows for several South Asian economies. In 2023, India received approximately 125 billion dollars in remittances, making it the top recipient country in the world by absolute volume. A significant share of these funds is directed toward household consumption, school fees, and medical expenses rather than productive investment, which limits their long-term multiplier effect on local economies. Economists note that remittance flows tend to remain stable or increase during periods of domestic economic stress, giving them a counter-cyclical role that distinguishes them from foreign direct investment.
seeds.expected: Remittances from overseas workers are South Asia's largest external financial inflow, providing a counter-cyclical income buffer for households, though most funds go to consumption rather than productive investment.

metadata.angle: an economic, social, or public-policy phenomenon

seeds.prompt: India's Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, enacted in 2005, gives every rural household a legal entitlement to one hundred days of wage employment per year on public works projects. The program is demand-driven: any adult who presents themselves at a government works site must be given employment within fifteen days or paid an unemployment allowance. Independent evaluations have found that the scheme reduced rural poverty rates in participating districts and narrowed the wage gap between men and women by setting a uniform minimum daily wage. Critics have identified persistent problems with delayed wage payments, ghost beneficiaries, and the concentration of works on road construction rather than durable community assets.
seeds.expected: India's MGNREGA guarantees rural households one hundred days of paid public work annually and has reduced poverty and gender wage gaps, but faces implementation problems including payment delays and suboptimal asset creation.

metadata.angle: an economic, social, or public-policy phenomenon

seeds.prompt: South Asian cities are growing faster than their infrastructure can expand, a pattern referred to as unplanned or informal urbanization. In cities such as Mumbai, Dhaka, and Karachi, between thirty and sixty percent of the urban population lives in informal settlements with insecure land tenure, inadequate sanitation, and unreliable electricity. Municipal governments collect insufficient property tax revenue to finance the roads, water systems, and transit networks that formal urban expansion requires, creating a feedback loop in which poor infrastructure discourages formal development. Research on comparable urbanization episodes in East Asia suggests that cities which invested early in public transit and land-use planning grew more equitably and avoided the deepest infrastructure deficits.
seeds.expected: Rapid and unplanned urbanization across South Asian cities has produced large informal settlements and a tax-revenue shortfall that prevents the infrastructure investment needed to support equitable urban growth.

metadata.angle: an economic, social, or public-policy phenomenon
